In the input grid, you will see multiple objects of different sizes and colors. Some of the objects will appear to be 'on top' and some will appear to be 'underneath' the other objects. If it helps, imagine they are pieces of paper and you are stacking them in physical space, to determine which are 'on top of' the stack. First, resize your grid to match the full size of the input grid. Now, you will begin to copy the objects into the grid. Copy all objects, but ALL objects must be one color only - the color of the object that is 'on top' in the input grid. You will end up with a single, larger shape in the color of the topmost object in the input grid, as though you had outlined all objects together and filled in the outline with the color of the 'upper' shape.

In the input grid, you will see multiple objects of different colors and sizes. Some objects will be 'over' or covering part of the other objects. (Imagine they are pieces of colored paper, cut out and laid over one another if this helps.) First, resize your grid to match the input grid. Now, begin to copy ALL objects into the grid in their exact positions. However, you will not be copying the COLORS on the input grid. You will be changing the colors of the objects so that ALL objects are the same color as the "top" object (or objects, the ones that would be laid on top if it were pieces of paper), so that when you are finished, you will have a single, larger object in your grid, that is the same color as the topmost layer object in the input grid. It will be as though you outlined all the objects in the input grid, then filled the outline with the color of the uppermost object.

In the input grid, you will see multiple, different objects of different colors and sizes. Some will be "layered" over the other objects (imagine they are cutout pieces of paper if you need to, then you will see that some will be laid on top of others). For the sake of the description, we will call the ones laid on top of others the "upper" objects. First, copy the size of the input grid. Now, begin to copy the objects onto the grid. You will copy all of the objects, in their exact grid locations. However, you will need to color ALL of the objects, regardless of their colors in the input grid, the same color as the "upper" object(s) in the input grid. When you are finished, your final piece will appear as though you traced the outline of all objects together, and then filled the outline with the color of the upper object(s).

The only thing you will change is the color - ALL objects must be the same color as the 'top layer' objects in the input grid.

Change the color of all objects to the color of the 'uppermost' objects in the input. All objects in your final shape should be a single color and one contiguous shape.
